title: Percentages
blurb: Master percent, change, reverse percent, interest, and profit/loss for GRE Quant.
theory:
What a Percent Means

A percent means "per hundred." So 25% literally means 25 out of 100, or the fraction 25/100. The percent sign (%) is just shorthand for ÷100. This single idea powers every percent problem on the GRE.

Converting Between Forms

You must switch fluidly between percents, decimals, and fractions.

  • Percent → Decimal: divide by 100 (move the point 2 places left). 45% = 0.45.
  • Decimal → Percent: multiply by 100 (move the point 2 places right). 0.7 = 70%.
  • Percent → Fraction: put it over 100 and simplify. 40% = 40/100 = 2/5.
  • Fraction → Percent: divide and multiply by 100. 3/8 = 0.375 = 37.5%.

Memorize the common ones: 1/2 = 50%, 1/3 ≈ 33.3%, 1/4 = 25%, 1/5 = 20%, 1/8 = 12.5%, 1/6 ≈ 16.7%.

Percent of a Number

"x% of N" = (x/100) × N. The word of almost always means multiply. Example: 30% of 80 = 0.30 × 80 = 24.

A useful tool is the percent equation: part = percent × whole. You can solve for any one of the three when given the other two.

FindFormula
Partpart = (percent/100) × whole
Percentpercent = (part/whole) × 100
Wholewhole = part ÷ (percent/100)

Percent Increase and Decrease

The percent change formula is the backbone of this topic:

percent change = (new − old) / old × 100

The denominator is always the original (old) value, never the new one. A positive result is an increase; a negative result is a decrease. Example: a price rises from 50 to 65. Change = (65 − 50)/50 × 100 = 15/50 × 100 = 30% increase.

Multiplier Method

Instead of computing the change and adding it back, multiply directly by a single factor:

  • Increase by p%: multiply by (1 + p/100). Up 20% = × 1.20.
  • Decrease by p%: multiply by (1 − p/100). Down 15% = × 0.85.

This is faster and avoids errors, especially for successive changes.

Successive Percent Changes

When changes happen one after another, you multiply the factors — you do NOT add the percents. Example: 100 increased 20% then decreased 20% = 100 × 1.20 × 0.80 = 96, not 100. The result is a 4% net decrease. A useful identity: up by a% then down by a% always gives a net change of −a2/100 percent.

Reverse Percent (Find the Original)

If a value is given after a percent change and you must find the original, divide by the multiplier — do not just take that percent of the new value. If a price after a 20% discount is 64, the original is 64 ÷ 0.80 = 80. Check: 80 × 0.80 = 64. ✓

Percent vs Percentage Points

This is a classic GRE trap. If an interest rate rises from 4% to 6%, it rose by 2 percentage points, but the percent increase is (6 − 4)/4 × 100 = 50%. "Points" measure the absolute gap; "percent change" measures the relative growth.

Discounts, Markups, Profit and Loss

  • Discount: sale price = list price × (1 − discount%).
  • Markup: selling price = cost × (1 + markup%).
  • Profit % = (selling price − cost) / cost × 100. Profit and loss are always measured relative to cost unless stated otherwise.
  • Loss % = (cost − selling price) / cost × 100.

Simple Interest

I = P × r × t, where P is principal, r is the rate per period (as a decimal), and t is the number of periods. The total amount is A = P + I = P(1 + rt). Simple interest is computed on the original principal only.

Compound Interest

Compound interest earns interest on previously earned interest. The amount after t periods is:

A = P × (1 + r)t

Example: 2000 at 10% compounded annually for 2 years = 2000 × (1.10)2 = 2000 × 1.21 = 2420. Note that compound interest always exceeds simple interest after the first period. If compounding happens n times per year, use A = P(1 + r/n)nt.
whatEtsTests:
• The percent change formula with the original value in the denominator — the single most tested idea.
  • Successive changes that must be multiplied, not added (the "up x% then down x%" trap).
  • Reverse-percent problems: given the final value, recover the original by dividing by the multiplier.
  • The distinction between percent change and percentage points.
  • Quantitative Comparison items where two percents look symmetric (e.g., a% of b vs b% of a).
  • Profit/loss and discount problems anchored to cost vs list price.
  • Simple vs compound interest, especially for small numbers of periods.
tips: x% of y always equals y% of x, so 18% of 50 = 50% of 18 = 9. Swap to make it easy.
Use multipliers: up 20% then down 30% is just ×1.20×0.70 = ×0.84, a 16% net drop.
To find a percent of a number, find 10% (move decimal one place) and 1%, then build up.
For reverse percent, DIVIDE by the multiplier: final after 25% off means original = final ÷ 0.75.
Memorize fraction equivalents (1/8=12.5%, 3/8=37.5%, 5/8=62.5%) to skip long division.
Up a% then down a% never returns to start; the net is always a loss of (a²/100)%.
traps: Putting the NEW value in the denominator of the percent change formula instead of the original.
Adding successive percent changes (20% then 20% ≠ 40%) instead of multiplying the factors.
Confusing percentage points with percent change (4% to 6% is 2 points but a 50% increase).
Taking the discount percent of the SALE price when finding the original — you must divide.
Computing profit percent on the selling price rather than on the cost.

examples.q: A jacket priced at 120 is marked down by 25%, and then the discounted price is reduced by a further 10% at checkout. What is the final price?
examples.steps:
1. Use multipliers. A 25% discount means ×(1 − 0.25) = ×0.75.
  2. A further 10% reduction means ×(1 − 0.10) = ×0.90.
  3. Final price = 120 × 0.75 × 0.90.
  4. 120 × 0.75 = 90; then 90 × 0.90 = 81.
  5. The final price is 81.
examples.shortcut: Combine the factors first: 0.75 × 0.90 = 0.675, so 120 × 0.675 = 81. The net discount is 32.5%.

examples.q: After a 20% increase, a population is 30,000. What was the population before the increase?
examples.steps:
1. This is a reverse-percent problem. The 30,000 is the value AFTER the increase.
  2. A 20% increase means the original was multiplied by 1.20.
  3. So original × 1.20 = 30,000.
  4. Original = 30,000 ÷ 1.20 = 25,000.
  5. Check: 25,000 × 1.20 = 30,000. ✓ The answer is 25,000.
examples.shortcut: Divide by 1.2 directly: 30,000 ÷ 1.2 = 25,000. Never take 20% of 30,000 — that gives the wrong original.

examples.q: An unemployment rate falls from 8% to 6%. By how many percentage points did it fall, and what was the percent decrease?
examples.steps:
1. Percentage points are the absolute gap: 8 − 6 = 2 percentage points.
  2. Percent decrease uses the change formula with the original (8) in the denominator.
  3. Percent decrease = (8 − 6) / 8 × 100 = 2/8 × 100 = 25%.
  4. So the rate dropped 2 percentage points, which is a 25% decrease.
examples.shortcut: 2/8 simplifies to 1/4 = 25% instantly. Keep "points" and "percent change" mentally separate.

examples.q: A shopkeeper buys an item for 200 and sells it for 250. What is the profit percent?
examples.steps:
1. Profit = selling price − cost = 250 − 200 = 50.
  2. Profit percent is measured relative to COST: profit% = profit / cost × 100.
  3. profit% = 50 / 200 × 100 = 25%.
  4. The profit is 25%.
examples.shortcut: 50/200 = 1/4 = 25%. Always divide profit by cost, not by selling price (which would give 20%).

questions.q: The fraction 7/20 is equal to what percent?
A value increases from 40 to 50. What is the percent increase?
questions.explain: Percent change = (new − old)/old × 100 = (50 − 40)/40 × 100 = 10/40 × 100 = 25%. The original (40) goes in the denominator.
25% of 60 = 0.25 × 60 = 15. 60% of 25 = 0.60 × 25 = 15. Since x% of y always equals y% of x, the two quantities are equal. The answer is C.

questions.q: A quantity of 120 is first increased by 30% and then the result is decreased by 30%. What is the final value?
questions.explain: Multiply factors: 120 × 1.30 × 0.70 = 120 × 0.91 = 109.2. The net effect is a 9% decrease (not 0%), because up a% then down a% loses a²/100 = 9%.

questions.q: After a 25% discount, the price of a coat is 90. What was the original price?
questions.explain: A 25% discount means the price was multiplied by 0.75. So original × 0.75 = 90, giving original = 90 ÷ 0.75 = 120. Check: 120 × 0.75 = 90. ✓

questions.q: A company's market share rose from 30% to 36% of the market.
questions.info: A company's market share rose from 30% to 36% of the market.
questions.qa: The increase in the company's market share, in percentage points
questions.qb: The percent increase in the company's market share
questions.explain: Quantity A (percentage points) = 36 − 30 = 6. Quantity B (percent increase) = (36 − 30)/30 × 100 = 6/30 × 100 = 20. Since 20 > 6, Quantity B is greater. The answer is B.

questions.q: An amount of 2,500 is invested at a simple annual interest rate of 4% for 3 years. How much interest (in dollars) is earned?
questions.explain: Simple interest I = P × r × t = 2500 × 0.04 × 3 = 2500 × 0.12 = 300. The interest earned is 300.

questions.q: Select all of the following that are equal to 0.6.
A merchant buys an item for 80 and sells it for 100. The profit, expressed as a percent of the cost, is what percent?
questions.explain: Profit = 100 − 80 = 20. Profit percent is relative to COST: 20/80 × 100 = 25%. (The trap answer 20% comes from dividing by the selling price 100.)

questions.q: 5,000 is invested at 20% annual interest compounded annually. What is the total amount after 2 years?
questions.explain: A = P(1 + r)^t = 5000 × (1.20)² = 5000 × 1.44 = 7,200. (Simple interest would give only 5000 + 2×1000 = 7,000; the extra 200 is interest on interest.)

questions.q: A positive number is increased by 25%, and then that result is decreased by 20%.
questions.info: A positive number is increased by 25%, and then that result is decreased by 20%.
questions.explain: Final = original × 1.25 × 0.80 = original × 1.00 = original. The two changes exactly cancel, so the final value equals the original number for any positive number. The answer is C.
